methods.description: This method involves estimating power spectra using Thomson's multitaper method with linear detrending and Gaussian smoothing to analyze climate data from ice cores.

steps.description: Missing years in the published records (∼ 1.6 % of all data points) are linearly interpolated.
steps.searchable_summary: interpolate missing data points in time series
inputs: time series data with missing points

steps.description: Power spectra are then estimated using Thomson's multitaper method with three windows with linear detrending before analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: estimate power spectra using multitaper method

steps.description: Spectral smoothing is necessary to improve the quality of the estimates from short time series. A Gaussian smoothing kernel with varying width proportional to the applied frequency is used.
steps.searchable_summary: apply Gaussian smoothing to power spectra

steps.description: The lowest frequency is omitted from all plots and calculations to avoid bias.

methods.description: This method demonstrates the analysis steps using the DML1 data set to derive power spectra and assess isotope variations.

steps.description: Each power spectrum derived from an individual record of the DML1 firn-core array provides a timescale-dependent representation of the isotope variations.
steps.searchable_summary: derive power spectrum for each DML1 record

steps.description: The mean spectrum, M, of all individual spectra reduces spectral uncertainty and provides a robust estimate of the single records' spectrum.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate mean spectrum from individual spectra

steps.description: The mean spectrum divided by the number of records (M/n) is the expected spectrum if all isotope variations were independent noise.
steps.searchable_summary: divide mean spectrum by number of records

steps.description: Averaging in the time domain across records results in a spectrum S, where the noise component is reduced by 1/n but the common signal remains.
steps.searchable_summary: average records in time domain to obtain spectrum S

methods.description: This method assesses the physical meaning of separating variability in Antarctic isotope records into local variations (noise) and spatially coherent variations (signal).

steps.description: Assess the spatial scales of major processes influencing isotopic composition: temperature variations, atmospheric circulation, precipitation intermittency, and stratigraphic noise.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze spatial scales of processes affecting isotopic composition

steps.description: Interpret estimated isotope signal spectra as temperature signals, considering decorrelation scales and other processes.
steps.searchable_summary: interpret isotope signal spectra as temperature signals
